title: The Customer Concentration Problem Buyers Find Before You Do
linkedin:
Two businesses. Same sector, same revenue, same three years of consistent growth. The buyer who saw both made very different offers.

The difference came down to one thing the accounts showed clearly once the client list was mapped against revenue. One business had a single client accounting for twenty-six percent of total income. The other had no client above twelve percent.

The first attracted a lower multiple. Not because it was poorly run. Because the buyer had to price what would happen if that one relationship changed.

The threshold most experienced buyers use is around twenty percent of revenue from any single client. Above that, they begin modelling what the business earns without it. The movement between four times and five times EBITDA on $500,000 profit is a $500,000 gap in the sale price, built into the offer before any negotiation begins.

What makes this worth understanding now is that addressing concentration takes two to three financial years to show credibly in the accounts. Buyers look at trend, not snapshot. One good year is noise. Three years of improving data is a business.

The practical work is not dramatic: formalise informal client relationships into documented agreements, develop smaller accounts that have been deprioritised, and ensure key contacts within large clients sit with the team rather than the owner personally.

July is the natural starting point. A full year of revenue and margin data is available. The FY27 planning window is open. The lead time before any intended exit is at its longest.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#BusinessValuation #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
If your biggest client walked out tomorrow, how much of your revenue would go with them?

Most business owners know the answer roughly. But have they actually mapped the percentage? Buyers run this calculation within the first few minutes of opening a set of accounts.

Customer concentration, one or two clients accounting for too much of what comes in, is one of the more overlooked factors in business valuation. From inside the business, a large client feels like a strength. From outside, it looks like a risk.

The start of a new financial year is the right moment to work out exactly where yours sits.

#BusinessValuation #AustralianBusiness
google:
One of the most consistent findings when we work with East Coast SME owners through a business valuation is the gap between what the profit and loss suggests the business is worth and what a buyer is prepared to offer once the client list is mapped.

The disconnect usually comes down to customer concentration: the degree to which revenue or profit depends on a small number of clients. When one client accounts for more than twenty percent of revenue, buyers build their offer around what happens if that relationship does not survive the change of ownership.

For business owners across Brisbane, the Gold Coast, Sydney, and Melbourne who are thinking about an eventual sale, whether that is two years away or six, the first step is simply knowing where your concentration currently sits. Most businesses do not have this mapped at the client level.

We have published a new piece on the blog this week covering how buyers measure concentration, the multiple discount it typically creates, and the practical work that reduces it over time. The new financial year is the natural moment to begin, while there is still lead time to influence the result.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
